questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of lesions on his forehead, nose, and cheeks. He has multiple open and closed comedones, a few scattered papules and pustules, but no nodules or cysts. He is otherwise well. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of comedones is pathognomonic for acne vulgaris. Rosacea typically occurs in adults >30 years and lacks comedones. Perioral dermatitis is perioral and perinasal with small papules. Folliculitis is often itchy and due to bacterial infection. Seborrhoeic dermatitis has greasy scales.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with multiple painful, deep-seated nodules and cysts on her face, chest, and back, with associated scarring. She has no fever, arthralgia, or ulcerations. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Nodulocystic acne is severe acne with large nodules and cysts, but without systemic symptoms. Acne fulminans is accompanied by fever and joint pain. Rosacea fulminans (pyoderma faciale) is explosive onset on face in young women with pustules but no comedones. Furunculosis and folliculitis are infectious and usually not confluent on back and chest with comedones.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with central facial erythema, telangiectasias, and small pustules on his nose and cheeks. He reports flushing after drinking red wine. He has no comedones. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Rosacea is characterized by centrofacial erythema, telangiectasias, and papulopustules without comedones, often triggered by alcohol. Acne vulgaris has comedones. Perioral dermatitis is perioral and spares the nose. Seborrhoeic dermatitis has greasy scales. Photodamage is not pustular.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with numerous superficial erosions, crusts, and scars on her face. She admits to picking at her skin. On close inspection, a few non-inflamed comedones are visible. She has no significant erythema or telangiectasias. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acne excoriée is a variant of acne vulgaris where patients pick at minor comedones leading to excoriations and scars. Rosacea lacks comedones. Eczema herpeticum is a disseminated herpes infection. Impetigo is a contagious bacterial infection with honey-colored crusts. Delusional parasitosis involves false belief of infestation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 14 weeks gestation presents with new-onset inflammatory papules and pustules on her chin and jawline. She has a few closed comedones and no systemic symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acne vulgaris commonly occurs or worsens in pregnancy due to hormonal changes. The presence of comedones points to acne. Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy usually appears in the third trimester and is intensely pruritic. Rosacea lacks comedones. Perioral dermatitis is perioral. Impetigo herpetiformis is a rare pustular psoriasis with fever.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of facial comedones and inflammatory papules. He is otherwise well with no systemic symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acne vulgaris is diagnosed clinically based on the presence of comedones and inflammatory lesions. No confirmatory tests are needed in typical cases. The other options are indicated only when there are atypical features such as signs of hyperandrogenism or infection.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with facial flushing and papules. She reports that spicy foods trigger the flushing and she has no comedones. Which of the following, if present, is a diagnostic criterion for rosacea?
questions.explanation: Telangiectasia is a recognised diagnostic criterion for rosacea (along with persistent erythema, papules/pustules, and ocular symptoms). Comedones are characteristic of acne, not rosacea; their absence is a key distinguishing feature.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old male presents with sudden onset of painful, ulcerative acne lesions on the chest and back, accompanied by fever, arthralgia, and leukocytosis. Which laboratory finding is most consistent with a diagnosis of acne fulminans?
questions.explanation: Acne fulminans is a severe variant with systemic features including fever and arthralgia. An elevated CRP (or ESR) is typical due to systemic inflammation. Other options are not specific: testosterone is normal, ANA is negative, and cultures are sterile.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known facial rosacea complains of eye irritation, redness, and a feeling of a foreign body. On examination, there is blepharitis and conjunctival injection. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm ocular involvement in this patient?
questions.explanation: Slit-lamp examination can reveal telangiectasia of the eyelid margins and other signs of ocular rosacea, which is primarily a clinical diagnosis. Schirmer's test measures tear production but is not diagnostic; fluorescein staining can show corneal damage but is not the first step.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with a history of severe acne treated with oral isotretinoin 6 months ago now has persistent facial redness and occasional flushing. She has no comedones or active papules. Which of the following, if present, would most strongly suggest a diagnosis of rosacea rather than post-inflammatory erythema?
questions.explanation: Telangiectasia is a hallmark of rosacea and is not seen in post-inflammatory erythema. Post-inflammatory erythema is typically flat, red, and without additional vascular changes. The presence of telangiectasia, even without papules, points to rosacea.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old female presents with mild facial acne. She has several comedones and a few inflamed papules. She has not tried any treatment before. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For mild acne, first-line is benzoyl peroxide alone or with a topical antibiotic/retinoid. Benzoyl peroxide is preferred as monotherapy due to antimicrobial and comedolytic effects with low resistance risk. Topical clindamycin alone is discouraged due to resistance. Oral antibiotics and isotretinoin are reserved for more severe disease.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old male presents with severe acne on his back and chest. There are multiple nodules and cysts, with significant scarring. He has not had any previous treatment. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Severe nodulocystic acne is an indication for oral isotretinoin as first-line therapy. Oral antibiotics are less effective for severe disease. Topical therapy alone is insufficient. Intralesional steroids can be used for individual cysts but not as primary therapy.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with persistent facial redness, and small papules and pustules on her cheeks and nose. There is no ocular discomfort. She has not used any treatment. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For mild-to-moderate papulopustular rosacea, first-line is topical metronidazole or azelaic acid. Topical ivermectin is an alternative but not first-line. Oral doxycycline is for more severe or resistant cases. Isotretinoin reserved for severe. Brimonidine only for erythema, not inflammatory lesions.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with rosacea presents with red, sore eyes, photophobia, and blurry vision. On examination, he has conjunctival injection, blepharitis, and small chalazia. There are mild papules on his face. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Ocular rosacea is an indication for oral tetracycline (e.g., doxycycline) due to its anti-inflammatory effects. Topical treatments are insufficient for ocular involvement. Isotretinoin is not first-line and can worsen dry eyes. Lubricants are supportive but not primary therapy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 14 weeks pregnant presents with mild inflammatory acne on her face. She has not tried any treatment. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Oral isotretinoin is teratogenic and absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. All other options are topical treatments considered safe for use in pregnancy for acne.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl presents to her GP with a 6-month history of facial acne. On examination, she has approximately 10 non-inflamed comedones and 5 small papules on her forehead and cheeks. No cysts or nodules. She has tried over-the-counter cleansers with no improvement. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a topical preparation containing benzoyl peroxide or a topical retinoid as first-line for mild acne. Oral antibiotics and hormonal therapy are reserved for moderate-to-severe disease. Referral is not needed at this stage.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents to the ED with a 2-week history of rapidly worsening acne. He has multiple painful nodules and cysts on his face, chest, and back, with some scarring. He has not tried any prescription treatments. He is systemically well, afebrile. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to dermatology for consideration of isotretinoin
Perform a skin swab for culture
questions.explanation: For severe acne without systemic symptoms, oral tetracycline (e.g., lymecycline) is first-line, often combined with a topical agent. Isotretinoin is reserved for treatment-resistant or severe nodulocystic acne after failure of oral antibiotics. Swabs are not routinely indicated.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with persistent facial redness and small papules on her cheeks and nose for several months. She reports occasional flushing after spicy food. There are no comedones. She has not used any treatments. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: This is typical papulopustular rosacea. First-line treatment is topical metronidazole or azelaic acid. Oral metronidazole is not first-line; oral isotretinoin is reserved for severe cases. Sun avoidance is adjunctive but not first-line treatment. Referral is not needed initially.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male is brought to the ED with a 3-day history of sudden-onset, painful, ulcerating nodules on his chest and back, with fever (38.5°C) and arthralgia. He has a history of severe acne but no prior treatment. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This is acne fulminans. Immediate treatment with oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) is indicated to control inflammation. Isotretinoin should be started only after steroids are established, typically under dermatology guidance. Antibiotics are not first-line; biopsy is not needed for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 12 weeks pregnant presents with mild facial acne consisting of comedones and a few inflammatory papules. She is concerned about using any medication. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Advise no treatment until after pregnancy
questions.explanation: Topical benzoyl peroxide is safe and effective in pregnancy. Oral tetracyclines (e.g., doxycycline) are contraindicated. Oral erythromycin can be used but is second-line due to GI side effects and potential for antibiotic resistance. Topical retinoids (tretinoin) are contraindicated. Treatment is appropriate to prevent scarring.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy presents with a 2-week history of sudden-onset, painful, nodular acne on his chest and back. He also reports fevers and joint aches. On examination, there are multiple inflamed nodules and ulcerations. What single finding in this presentation most strongly suggests a severe systemic variant that requires urgent escalation?
options: Distribution on trunk rather than face
Previous mild acne that worsened suddenly
questions.explanation: Fever and arthralgias indicate systemic involvement, characteristic of acne fulminans, an emergency requiring immediate referral. While all other options are common in severe acne, only systemic symptoms distinguish fulminans from simple nodulocystic acne.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known rosacea complains of persistent gritty sensation in both eyes, photophobia, and blurred vision. She has had occasional facial flushing and papules. Which clinical feature in this scenario is a red flag for sight-threatening ocular involvement?
questions.explanation: Gritty sensation and photophobia suggest keratitis or blepharitis from ocular rosacea, which can lead to corneal damage if untreated. Other options are typical of cutaneous rosacea without ocular emergency.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with no prior skin problems develops moderate acne on her face and upper chest over 3 months. She also notes increased facial hair and irregular periods. What finding in her history most strongly suggests an underlying endocrine abnormality that requires further investigation?
options: Failed response to over-the-counter topical treatments
questions.explanation: Irregular periods point to possible polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a common cause of adult-onset acne. Other options are not specific for endocrine disease; onset after 20 is simply a feature of adult acne.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man presents with severe nodulocystic acne affecting his face and back. He has already had several deep scars from previous lesions. He mentions his father had similar acne and developed raised, thickened scars. Which single finding in this patient's personal or family history should raise concern for a complication that may alter treatment decisions?
options: Age of the patient (19 years old)
Family history of raised, thickened scars
Presence of scars from previous acne
questions.explanation: Family history of raised scars indicates keloid predisposition. This affects management (e.g., avoid incision, early treatment) and is the key red flag. Scars alone are common; back involvement and age are not specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing rosacea has developed a bulbous, thickened, irregular appearance of his nose over several years. He has occasional flushing but no eye symptoms. What is the diagnosis that this finding represents a complication of rosacea?
questions.explanation: Rhinophyma is a recognised complication of rosacea, characterised by sebaceous gland hyperplasia and fibrosis leading to nasal enlargement. It is a cosmetic and functional concern but not an acute emergency; however, it is a distinct diagnosis to recognise.
